A company with 90% annual contract mix and a reported 1.5% monthly churn may actually face a 25–35% annual churn cliff — the kind of number that would kill a deal if stated honestly.

annual-plan-churn-risk.__TR_annual-plan-churn-risk_59__: — a flat or growing MRR line is easier to show when churned customers can't actually cancel yet.

hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_17__: Every SaaS seller believes their churn is "industry-leading." Every buyer who takes the CIM number at face value gets burned. The gap between reported churn and real churn is filled with methodological choices that happen to flatter the seller. Here are the seven most common ones — and how Churn Lens catches each one automatically.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_20__: The seller counts trial signups as "new customers" for top-line storytelling, then excludes trials that don't convert from the churn calculation. The result: signups look healthy, churn looks low, and nobody tracks the trial-to-paid conversion rate as a churn metric.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_22__: Request a list of ALL subscription records including trials with $0 MRR. If trials are excluded from the data export, assume this trick is in play. Churn Lens flags any gap between signed-up accounts and paid accounts.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_24__: The seller shows churn for a "representative" cohort — usually the best-performing month. If January 2025 had 2% churn but the trailing 12-month average is 5%, the cohort is cherry-picked.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_26__: Ask for churn by month for the last 24 months, not a single representative month. Churn Lens computes rolling 12-month averages that smooth out seasonal noise.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_28__: A customer who drops from the $200/mo plan to the $50/mo plan isn't counted as churned (they're still a customer). But $150/mo of revenue just disappeared. The seller reports 0% logo churn while revenue churn quietly eats 10% of MRR/year.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_30__: Always compute BOTH logo churn AND revenue churn. A gap between the two that widens over time is a downgrade signal. Churn Lens surfaces revenue churn side-by-side with logo churn.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_32__: A 5% monthly churn rate sounds high. A 46% annual churn rate (compounded) also sounds high. But a seller who quotes "~35% annual churn" using a simplified linear projection is understating by 11 percentage points. This trick works because most buyers don't do the compounding math.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_34__: Always convert monthly churn to annual yourself: (1 - monthly_churn_rate)^12. Churn Lens shows both monthly and annual churn in every report.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_36__: "We only count voluntary cancellations." Sellers who exclude failed payments, expired cards, and billing issues can cut their reported churn by 20-40%. But the revenue is still gone.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_38__: Ask for the split between voluntary and involuntary churn. If the seller doesn't track it, they're not running a real retention operation — and you'll inherit the problem.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_40__: When a customer churns in April, reactivates in June, and churns again in August, the seller counts only one churn event. The net churn number looks stable, but the MRR is bouncing like a ping-pong ball.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_42__: Analyze churn at the subscription-ID level, not the customer-ID level. Each cancellation event counts. Churn Lens flags customers with serial churn/reactivation patterns.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_44__: High gross churn masked by even higher new sales. The reported churn rate stays flat or even declines, but only because new customers flood the denominator. When growth stalls (as it inevitably does post-acquisition), the churn that was always there becomes visible.
hidden-churn-saas-acquisition.__TR_hidden-churn-saas-acquisition_46__: Compare gross churn (cancellations / beginning-of-period customers) to net churn (net loss / beginning-of-period). If they diverge significantly, the business is masking churn with growth. Churn Lens computes both and highlights the gap.

How to Evaluate a SaaS Before Buying
A six-step buyer-side framework for evaluating any SaaS acquisition target. Move from headline metrics to durable revenue analysis — the difference between a good deal and a value trap.

Evaluating a SaaS business for acquisition requires looking past the seller's narrative and into the raw subscription data. This framework walks through the six analyses that separate healthy, durable revenue from fragile numbers that will collapse post-close.
revenue. Headline MRR is not durable revenue. Durable revenue is MRR from customers who are actively using the product, on appropriate plans, with healthy engagement patterns.
What percentage of MRR comes from actively engaged customers?
Is expansion revenue growing or shrinking as a percentage of total?
Are there pricing cliffs where customers on legacy plans pay dramatically less than new customers?
For a deep dive, read our
Churn is the single most important metric in SaaS valuation, but sellers routinely present it in the most flattering light. You need both
Logo churn tells you breadth of customer dissatisfaction
Revenue churn tells you financial impact of departures
When revenue churn exceeds logo churn, your largest customers are leaving — a concentration signal
When logo churn exceeds revenue churn, small customers leave but expansion revenue masks the loss
— a 3% monthly churn means very different things in enterprise SaaS vs. SMB e-commerce apps. Also read about the
Concentration risk determines whether the loss of a single customer is a survivable event or a business-ending one. Calculate the
from the subscription CSV and identify any "whale" customers above 5% of MRR.
Whale customers on monthly contracts with no lock-in
If more than 50% of MRR is on annual contracts, monthly churn metrics are heavily distorted. You need to map the renewal calendar:
How much MRR is exposed in any given month?
What's the historical annual renewal rate?
Are there renewal cliffs where multiple contracts expire simultaneously?
Cohort analysis reveals whether customer quality is improving or deteriorating over time. Group customers by signup month and track their revenue retention curve:
Are newer cohorts retaining better or worse than older ones?
Is there a "cohort cliff" at month 3, 6, or 12 where retention drops sharply?
Do cohorts from specific acquisition channels perform differently?
Is expansion revenue within cohorts growing (good) or flat/declining (concerning)?
Declining cohort retention is one of the strongest
— it means the product is losing product-market fit, even if headline growth still looks positive due to new customer acquisition.
Finally, assess whether the growth trajectory is sustainable. Key signals:
Is new customer acquisition growing, flat, or declining?
How much of growth depends on paid acquisition vs. organic/word-of-mouth?
Is NRR above 100% (expansion exceeds churn) or below?
Does one acquisition channel drive most new signups?

A business growing 5% month-over-month with negative net revenue retention is fragile — it's growing despite losing existing revenue. A business with flat MRR but 110% NRR is durable — it's retaining and expanding even without new acquisition.

Upload the target's subscription CSV and Churn Lens runs all six analyses automatically — revenue quality, logo churn, revenue churn, concentration (HHI), annual plan risk, and cohort trends. Get a buyer-side risk report in minutes.
Putting It Together: The Go/No-Go Decision
After completing all six analyses, you should be able to answer one question with confidence:
What is the durable revenue base, and what multiple of it am I willing to pay?
Durable revenue = Total MRR − Zombie MRR − Concentration-adjusted risk buffer − Annual cliff exposure. Only this number should drive your valuation, not the headline MRR the seller quotes.

inactive-paid-accounts.__TR_inactive-paid-accounts_17__: MRR is only real if customers are actually using the product. A surprising number of SaaS businesses carry "zombie MRR" — active subscriptions where the customer has effectively abandoned the tool but hasn't cancelled yet. This revenue looks stable on the P&L but is one invoice away from disappearing.
inactive-paid-accounts.__TR_inactive-paid-accounts_19__: Zombie MRR is recurring revenue from accounts that are paying but no longer engaging with the product. These customers haven't churned in the billing sense — the card still gets charged every month — but they've churned in every meaningful sense: no logins, no usage, no support tickets, no expansion. They're paying out of inertia, forgetfulness, or because cancellation is friction-heavy.
inactive-paid-accounts.__TR_inactive-paid-accounts_20__: For buyers, zombie MRR is dangerous because it inflates both the headline MRR number and the implied retention rate. A business reporting $80K MRR with 15% zombie MRR is really a $68K MRR business with a retention problem masked by slow cancellation behavior. When those zombies finally cancel — and they will — the churn rate spikes and the buyer is left holding the decay.

mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_1__: Raw MRR is a vanity metric. Learn what revenue quality actually means — net revenue retention, expansion sustainability, concentration adjustment, and durable MRR.

mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_3__: Why raw MRR is a vanity metric and how to calculate true revenue quality for SaaS acquisitions.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_4__: MRR vs Revenue Quality: Why Headline MRR Misleads Buyers
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_5__: Why raw MRR is a vanity metric and how to calculate true revenue quality for SaaS acquisitions.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_16__: $50K MRR sounds great — until you learn 15% is zombie revenue, 30% is concentrated in three customers, and 40% is on annual plans renewing next quarter. Here's how to separate vanity MRR from durable revenue.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_17__: Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) is the headline number every SaaS seller quotes. It's also the number most likely to mislead a buyer. MRR tells you how much revenue is billed this month — it says nothing about how much of that revenue will still exist in 6, 12, or 18 months. Revenue quality is the answer to that question.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_18__: Why MRR Is a Vanity Metric
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_19__: MRR counts every active subscription at its current billing rate. It makes no distinction between:
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_20__: A customer who logged in today and is actively expanding their usage
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_21__: A customer who hasn't opened the product in 90 days but hasn't cancelled yet
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_22__: A customer who represents 25% of total MRR and could leave at any time
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_23__: A customer on a legacy discounted plan paying 60% less than market rate
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_24__: All four show up identically in the MRR number. But they represent vastly different levels of revenue durability.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_26__: Revenue quality is a composite assessment of how durable and trustworthy the MRR number is. It accounts for five factors:
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_28__: What percentage of MRR from period N is still present in period N+12, excluding expansion? This is the floor — the worst-case retention scenario. Healthy SaaS: 85-95%.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_30__: Same as GRR but including expansion revenue. Above 100% means existing customers are growing faster than churn. This is the gold standard metric.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_32__: GRR/NRR adjusted for customer concentration risk. If your top customer represents 30% of MRR, effective retention is lower because their departure has outsized impact.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_34__: MRR weighted by product engagement levels. Inactive accounts (
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_38__: Is expansion coming from genuine upsell (more seats, higher tier) or from price increases that customers may resist at renewal?
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_39__: Net Revenue Retention: The Metric That Matters
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_40__: NRR is the single best summary metric for revenue quality. It measures what happened to the revenue from customers you had 12 months ago:
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_41__: NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion MRR − Churned MRR − Contraction MRR) / Starting MRR
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_61__: Upload the target's subscription data and Churn Lens automatically computes GRR, NRR, concentration-adjusted retention, engagement-weighted MRR, and a composite Revenue Quality Score. See the true revenue picture in minutes.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_64__: Expansion revenue (upsells, seat additions, plan upgrades) can make NRR look great while masking underlying problems. Watch for:
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_66__: Revenue grows because prices increased, not because customers gained value. This is unsustainable — eventually customers churn due to price.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_68__: A large enterprise customer keeps adding seats — but those seats are unused. This is just delayed concentration risk.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_70__: Customers upgraded because old tiers were deprecated. They may not renew at the new price.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_71__: Genuine expansion comes from customers getting more value and naturally scaling their usage. Check whether expansion correlates with engagement growth — if MRR is rising but login frequency isn't, the expansion may be artificial.
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_72__: Durable MRR: The Number You Should Value
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_73__: After adjusting for all quality factors, you arrive at
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_75__: — the revenue you can reasonably expect to persist for 12+ months post-acquisition:
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_76__: Durable MRR = Total MRR − Zombie MRR − Concentration Risk Buffer − Annual Cliff Exposure
mrr-vs-revenue-quality.__TR_mrr-vs-revenue-quality_77__: This is the number that should drive your acquisition valuation. It's typically 70-90% of headline MRR for healthy SaaS, and can be as low as 50-60% for problematic targets. For the full scoring methodology, see our

saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_17__: Most SaaS acquisitions that go wrong share the same warning signs. The problem isn't that buyers can't see them — it's that they don't know where to look. Here are 12 red flags detectable from raw subscription data, ranked by severity. Any single flag warrants investigation; three or more is a strong signal to walk away or significantly reprice.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_19__: The seller reports monthly churn of 2% — but they're excluding trial cancellations, counting downgrades as "plan changes" rather than churn, or only counting customers who explicitly cancelled (missing failed-payment churn). Always recalculate churn from raw CSV data.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_22__: If the top 3 customers represent more than 40% of MRR, the business has a single-point-of-failure risk. One whale departure can wipe out a year of growth. Calculate the
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_26__: NRR trending downward over 3+ consecutive quarters means existing customers are worth less every month. Even if headline MRR is growing (thanks to new acquisition), the revenue base is rotting from within.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_29__: If 50%+ of MRR is on annual plans and a cluster of renewals hits the same month, you're buying a revenue cliff. The low monthly churn looks great — until renewal day.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_32__: 10-20% of customers haven't logged in for 90+ days but are still paying. This revenue is statistically certain to cancel within months. Subtract it from MRR before valuing the business.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_35__: If one channel (e.g., Google Ads, a single affiliate, one integration marketplace) drives 70%+ of new signups, the business is one algorithm change or partnership termination away from zero growth. Diversified acquisition is essential for durable revenue.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_37__: If the founder is the sole salesperson, handles all key customer relationships, or is the only person who understands the codebase, the business loses significant value on day one post-acquisition. Check whether customer relationships are institutionalized or personal.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_39__: If customers who signed up 6 months ago retain worse than customers who signed up 12 months ago, the product is losing product-market fit. New features, market shifts, or competitive pressure are eroding value. Always
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_42__: MRR is rising but login frequency, feature usage, or API calls are flat or declining. This means expansion revenue is coming from price increases or seat inflation, not genuine value growth. It's unsustainable and will reverse at renewal.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_43__: High Growth Fueled Entirely by New Acquisition
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_44__: If gross revenue retention is below 80% but the business is still growing, it means new customer acquisition is masking a leaking bucket. The moment acquisition slows (budget cut, channel saturation), MRR collapses. Check
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_46__: Pricing Below Market with No Path to Increase
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_47__: The SaaS charges significantly below market rate — and customers are locked into legacy pricing. Post-acquisition, price increases will trigger massive churn. The "growth opportunity" of raising prices is actually a churn bomb.
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_48__: Inconsistent Billing or Data Quality Issues
saas-acquisition-red-flags.__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_49__: If the subscription CSV has duplicate records, inconsistent plan naming, missing signup dates, or unexplained MRR fluctuations, the data integrity is questionable. Sellers who can't provide clean data are either disorganized or hiding something. Both are reasons to discount heavily or walk away.

saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_16__: 19 critical checks before you buy a SaaS business. Organized by risk domain — churn, concentration, revenue quality, unit economics, technical debt, and legal.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_17__: Every SaaS acquisition looks good in the CIM. The deal memo hides the landmines. This checklist forces you to look at each one — before you sign.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_21__: What percentage of customers cancel each month? Benchmark against sector medians. Anything above 5% monthly churn for B2B SaaS requires a structural reason (clear intent, seasonal business). Ask for the raw data, not a calculated metric.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_24__: NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion – Churn) / Starting MRR. An NRR >100% means expansion offsets churn — the asset grows itself. Below 85% means the company is slowly dying. Demand the calculation method.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_27__: A single churn number hides cohort decay. Newer cohorts that churn faster than old ones indicate product-market fit erosion, competitive pressure, or a degrading onboarding experience. Request cohort tables by quarter of acquisition.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_30__: Involuntary churn (failed payments, expired cards) is fixable. Voluntary churn (cancelled because the product wasn't valuable enough) is structural. If voluntary churn exceeds 60% of total, the value prop needs work — and you'll inherit that work.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_34__: If the top 5 customers represent more than 30% of MRR, you're buying a services business with a SaaS wrapper — not a product company. Losing one customer permanently changes the valuation basis. Calculate it yourself from raw CSV data.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_37__: If 70% of MRR comes from "Enterprise" tier customers, the revenue base is fragile during an acquisition transition. Enterprise customers churn less but churn harder when they do (more revenue per lost logo).
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_40__: Is revenue concentrated in one vertical or geography? A single GDPR regulatory shift, currency fluctuation, or sector downturn can wipe 40%+ of MRR overnight.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_44__: Flat or declining MRR over 12 months is the #1 deal-killer signal we see in Churn Lens analyses. Ask for month-by-month MRR for the last 24 months and look for inflection points (price changes, product launches, churn waves).
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_47__: Expansion revenue sounds good until you realize it's masking customers downgrading plans. Contraction (downgrades + plan reductions) tells you if the core value prop is eroding. Request downgrade events separately from churn.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_50__: The average time between signup and first payment tells you whether the product delivers quick value. Long payment velocity combined with high churn is a double signal: the product doesn't hook users fast enough.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_54__: A 3:1 LTV/CAC is the SaaS baseline. Below 3:1 means the company loses money on each customer over its lifetime. Above 5:1 means they may be under-investing in growth. Calculate from raw data — don't accept the seller's blended number.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_57__: How many months of gross margin does it take to recover customer acquisition costs? Over 18 months is risky for a bootstrapped acquisition. Under 6 months and the pricing may be too low.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_60__: A declining gross margin trend signals infrastructure bloat, rising cloud costs, or increasing support load per customer. Stable or improving margins are a green flag.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_64__: Check the dependency tree age, upgrade cadence, and whether there's automated testing. A stovepiped codebase with no CI/CD and a single developer key-man risk can add 6–12 months to your post-acquisition integration plan.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_67__: Is the product running because one person knows the infrastructure? A founder-run micro-SaaS that's never been documented will crater within 90 days of the founder leaving. Look at commit authorship distribution.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_70__: A serverless app tightly coupled to a single cloud provider's services is hard to migrate. Factor migration costs into your valuation model.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_74__: GDPR, CCPA, and sector-specific regulations change ownership obligations if you acquire. Check whether the current ToS allows assignment, and whether data processing agreements are in place with every customer.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_77__: Contractors without signed IP assignments, open-source license violations in the codebase, or trademark gaps in key markets can all surface post-close. Verify all contributor agreements.
saas-due-diligence-checklist.__TR_saas-due-diligence-checklist_80__: Every SaaS the business relies on (Stripe, AWS, SendGrid, OpenAI) is a risk if the plan is unsignable. Check change-of-control clauses in vendor contracts.

saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_18__: The Three Types of Revenue Decay
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_20__: MRR that's flat for 6+ months with no seasonal explanation is the most obvious decay signal. But even "growing" MRR can be decaying — see #3 below.
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_22__: Newer customer cohorts that churn faster than older cohorts indicate product-market fit erosion, onboarding degradation, or competitive pressure. This is a leading indicator — it shows up 6–12 months before MRR starts dropping.
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_24__: The most dangerous form of decay. Gross churn runs at 5% monthly, but new sales at 7% monthly makes MRR look like it's growing 2%. The business is actually a leaky bucket that runs on a treadmill: stop adding new customers and the revenue collapses. Churn Lens computes the "organic MRR" — the MRR you'd have without any new sales — to expose this.
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_26__: A $2.1M ARR target showed 3% month-over-month MRR growth. Churn Lens computed organic MRR: the company was losing 4.2% of revenue every month to churn and contraction. Without $73K/month in new sales, the business would lose half its revenue in 14 months. The buyer re-priced the deal with a 2.1× multiple instead of 4.5×.

saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_33__: — revenue lost to downgrades and plan reductions, expressed as a percentage of starting MRR
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_35__: — retention rate by acquisition cohort (quarterly), with trend line
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_37__: — the real acquisition multiple when you discount for expected revenue decay post-close
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_39__: These decay signals should trigger a hard pause:
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_41__: — the business is structurally shrinking and new sales can't keep pace
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_42__: Cohort retention is worsening for 4+ consecutive quarters
saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_43__: — this is a structural product/market problem, not a seasonal blip

saas-mrr-decline-analysis.__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_47__: — the business is a treadmill, not an asset

saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_17_: A SaaS business with 3% monthly logo churn looks healthy on paper — until you realize a single customer accounts for 35% of MRR. Revenue concentration is the most common way churn risk hides during acquisition diligence. The headline number is fine; the underlying fragility is lethal.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_19_: Revenue concentration measures how much of your recurring revenue depends on a small number of customers. A business where the top 10 accounts make up 20% of MRR is diversified. A business where the top 10 accounts make up 70% of MRR is one negotiation away from a crisis.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_20_: For SaaS acquirers, concentration is distinct from churn — but the two interact. High concentration amplifies the impact of every lost logo. If your top customer represents 40% of MRR and they churn, your effective annual revenue churn isn't 3% — it's 43% overnight.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_22_: The cleanest way to quantify concentration is the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), borrowed from antitrust economics. For each customer, calculate their share of total MRR (as a percentage), square it, and sum across all customers:
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_24_: Higher HHI means more concentration. Here's how to read the result for a SaaS acquisition:
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_45_: For context: a perfectly diversified business with 1,000 customers paying identical MRR has an HHI of 1,000 (each customer = 0.1%, squared = 0.0001, × 1,000). A business with 10 equal customers has an HHI of 10,000. Most healthy SaaS targets sit between 500 and 1,500.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_46_: The Whale Customer Problem in Acquisitions
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_47_: A "whale" is any single customer representing more than 15–20% of MRR. Whales distort every metric a buyer cares about:
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_49_: — a whale on a 3-year contract suppresses monthly churn for 36 months, then explodes the annual churn number the year they leave.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_51_: — one whale expanding by $30K/month can mask 50 small accounts churning the same amount.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_53_: — a single upsell to a whale can generate a quarter of the year's new revenue, hiding weak product-market fit in the long tail.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_54_: Sellers rarely disclose whale risk proactively. They highlight the big logo as a "marquee customer" and a "land-and-expand success story." It's both of those things — and a single point of failure.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_55_: How to Detect Hidden Concentration Before You Buy
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_56_: During diligence, request the customer-level MRR export (not a summary). Then:
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_57_: Calculate the top-5, top-10, and top-20 MRR share.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_58_: If the top 5 exceeds 40%, flag it. If the top 10 exceeds 60%, it's a structural risk.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_59_: Check contract length for each whale.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_60_: A whale on month-to-month billing is a ticking bomb. A whale on a 3-year contract with 18 months remaining gives you runway to diversify.
saas-revenue-concentration-risk.__TR_saas-revenue-concentration-risk_62_: A whale acquired 3 months before the sale process is a red flag — sellers sometimes "buy" a big logo to inflate the headline number.
